annotations.text: the loss of weight makes me consider the lesion a cancer until it has been proved otherwise.
The differential diagnosis of the hepatic lesion is long, because the imaging characteristics are not clear-cut.
if it had been involved, the patient would probably have had symptoms of gastric-outlet obstruction.
The appearance is also not consistent with a pyogenic abscess, which may be multicentric or have a large central cavitating area.
These characteristics are reminiscent of a hemangioma, perhaps a cavernous hemangioma.
Although metastases may be heterogeneous, they are not associated with rashes, except in the case of glucagonomas, which produce necrolytic migratory erythema.

Since the CT and MRI scans did not show any decrease in the volume of the right hepatic lobe or change in the intensity of the hepatic parenchyma, as compared with the kidney, and the results of liver-function tests were normal, it is improbable that this patient had cirrhosis.
Among malignant tumors, hepatocellular carcinoma, its fibrolamellar variant, peripheral cholangiocarcinoma, angiosarcoma, and primary hepatic lymphoma are the most likely possibilities.
Were the skin lesions associated with the liver lesion in this case?
I favor the diagnosis of fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma.
The description of the skin lesions suggests psoriasis, which is often confined to the hands and elbows and is characterized by scaling plaques.
The tumor was well circumscribed, and the surrounding uninvolved liver tissue was unremarkable.

The high resectability rate, the well-differentiated nature of the tumor, the young age of the patients, and the absence of both liver disease and systemic disease all contribute to a better prognosis for patients with these tumors.

The microscopical features described in this case clearly distinguish the tumor from focal nodular hyperplasia, in which bile ducts are often prominent, the hepatocytes are small and regular, and mitotic figures are infrequent.
Fibrosis is rare in hepatocellular carcinomas, occurring only in the fibrolamellar subtype and in the even rarer sclerosing hepatocellular carcinoma.

Intracellular and extracellular bodies characterized by smooth-edged cytoplasmic rarefaction, or pale bodies, are well described in fibrolamellar carcinoma and are seen only rarely in other tumors, but hyaline globules, which are common in other hepatic tumors, are seen as well.

Some authors have reported evidence of neurosecretory differentiation in fibrolamellar carcinoma and have raised the question of the neuroendocrine nature of these tumors ; others have suggested that a small subgroup of these tumors are oncocytic hepatic carcinoid tumors.
